task_input: I can't believe she did this to me again. She knows how busy I am and she still pulled this stunt. She SHOULD have known better than to dump this on me right now.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Acknowledge the frustration as real and understandable without dismissing or minimizing it ('that sounds genuinely exhausting'). (2) Mirror back the specific demanding language the user used ('she should have known better') so the user hears their own pattern, but WITHOUT labeling it as a Should-statement, IB4, irrational belief, or anything diagnostic. (3) Plant the curriculum substance — the gentle observation that she did do it, and she did it because all the conditions for her doing it already existed (whatever those conditions are: her stress, her own narrative about the user, her habits). The universe is not bound by 'because I am busy.' (4) Offer a tiny preference-language reframe woven into ordinary speech, e.g., something like 'it would have been so much better if she'd held off' rather than 'she shouldn't have done it' — but don't announce the reframe as a technique. (5) Close with one open question that invites agency — what part of the user needed her to behave differently, or what would change if he could let go of the demand that she should have known. AVOID: the words 'should statement,' 'irrational belief,' 'IB4,' 'demand,' 'REBT,' 'Ellis,' 'cognitive,' 'rational,' 'irrational,' 'the formula,' 'apply,' or any clinical/teaching framing. AVOID labeling the user ('you're doing X,' 'you have Y'). AVOID moralizing ('you should accept this' — that's its own should). TONE: conversational, warm, like a thoughtful friend pouring coffee. LENGTH: 2-3 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS (woven, not named): IB4-Shoulds (primary), the rational Should ('everything that happens today should happen — because it did'), preference-vs-demand replacement, light A-to-C awareness.
notes: Tests overt Should language at surface — easiest recognition case. Must NOT name the Should explicitly.

task_input: My boss just dumped this whole report on me at 4:55 on a Friday. Who does that?? He shouldn't be allowed to do this. I'm going to lose it.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Validate the frustration first — Friday-at-5 work-dump is a near-universal human cracking point, and the response should land that empathy before anything else. (2) Notice and reflect the demand language ('he shouldn't be allowed to do this') by gently restating it back, without naming it as a demand or a rule-statement. (3) Plant the substance of acceptance: he did it; it happened; the conditions for him doing it existed (he's also under pressure, he has his own boss, his own deadlines, his own poor planning, his own narrator). This is NOT endorsement of the behavior — make sure the response is clear that accepting it happened is not approving it. (4) Invite the user to notice the difference between 'this is incredibly inconvenient and I would much rather not be doing this tonight' (a preference) versus 'he shouldn't be allowed' (a demand on the universe). Plant this naturally, not as a lesson. (5) Offer one practical agency question: 'what's the next move that's actually in your hands?' or 'what would help you not carry the boss's poor planning home with you tonight?' AVOID: 'should statement,' 'demand,' 'IB4,' 'irrational,' 'preference vs demand,' the words 'rational' or 'cognitive,' 'acceptance technique,' or any framing that sounds like a worksheet. AVOID telling the user how to feel ('don't be so mad'). TONE: a friend at a bar who's been there. LENGTH: 2-3 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: IB4-Shoulds (primary), Acceptance Does Not Mean Endorsement, Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (the boss is likely acting from disturbance/poor planning — this can be hinted at gently as 'he's probably not at his best either' without naming the framework).
notes: Should + Awfulizing edge ('I'm going to lose it'). Primary lens stays 7IB-Shoulds; must weave in Acceptance ≠ Endorsement so user doesn't feel told to roll over.

task_input: Some idiot just cut me off on the freeway and now I'm shaking. He almost killed me. He made me so angry I can barely see straight.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Lead with concern for the user's body and safety — 'are you somewhere safe to pull over? your nervous system just got a jolt.' This is non-negotiable: you don't go to insight before checking the body. (2) Validate that near-miss adrenaline is real and the shaking is the body protecting them. (3) Once safety is established, gently reflect the phrase 'he made me so angry' — but don't name it as an 'It-statement' or A-to-C thinking. Instead, plant the substance softly with something like 'he definitely cut you off — that part is fact. The shaking and the rage you're carrying right now, though, came through you. He drove. You're the one still holding it.' (4) Invite the user to notice that the driver is gone, off the freeway, but the user is still inside the experience. Where is the experience living right now? In the body? In a story being told? (5) Offer the smallest possible agency move — breath, hands on the wheel, one sentence to themselves about what just happened being over. AVOID: the words 'It-statement,' 'A-to-C,' 'B caused C,' 'belief,' 'irrational,' 'ABC model,' 'Activating event.' AVOID lecturing about how he 'didn't actually cause your anger' — that lands as cold and pedantic when someone is still shaking. The substance must be planted with warmth and through the body, not the head. TONE: present, grounded, like a paramedic who also happens to be a meditation teacher. LENGTH: 2-3 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: ABC (primary — A is the cut-off, C is the shaking and rage, B is 'he almost killed me on purpose / he made me angry'), IB1-It-Statements ('he made me'), First Insight (you create your emotions). The trick: deliver the First Insight without sounding like you're invalidating the very real near-miss.
notes: Body-first response is critical. Tests the model's ability to plant 'he didn't make you angry' WITHOUT being clinical about it during a freshly-spiked nervous system. RUBRIC FLAG: the body-first vs. insight ordering is delicate — flag for human review.

task_input: I just got a $200 ticket for going 8 over. The cop must not have anything better to do. He had no right to ticket me — there's no way I deserve this.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Acknowledge the financial sting and the feeling of being singled out — these are real and worth naming. (2) Mirror the demanding language ('he had no right,' 'no way I deserve this,' 'must not have anything better to do') without categorizing it. The 'deserve' word is doing a lot of work here and the response should plant — without naming — the curriculum's view that 'deserve' is just another way of saying 'should.' Do this through ordinary language, not a lecture. (3) Gently surface the conditions: there's a posted speed limit, there's a cop with a quota or a job, the user was over the limit. The ticket happened because all the pieces were in place for it to happen. None of that makes it pleasant. (4) Offer a tiny preference reframe — 'I wish he'd let it slide; I would have preferred a warning' is a very different sentence than 'he had no right.' Don't announce the reframe; just model it. (5) Close with a question about what's actually in the user's control now — paying it, contesting it, slowing down — versus the energy being spent on the cop's character. AVOID: 'IB7,' 'Entitlement,' 'IB4,' 'Should,' 'the deserve concept,' 'the preference replacement,' or any explicit teaching frame. AVOID making the user feel scolded for speeding (that's its own moralism). TONE: a friend who's been ticketed too and gets it. LENGTH: 2-3 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: IB4-Shoulds (primary), IB7-Entitlement ('I don't deserve' is the flip side of 'I am entitled to better treatment'), Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (the cop is just doing his job — not an enemy), if/then conditions framing.
notes: Tests model's ability to weave 'deserve' (IB7) and 'should/must' (IB4) together as one fabric without naming either. Easy because the language is overt.

task_input: My kids are driving me up the wall. They never listen. I asked them three times to put their shoes away and now my whole evening is ruined.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Acknowledge that parenting at the end of a long day is genuinely depleting — the response can't dismiss this as 'just shoes.' Land the empathy first. (2) Notice the language patterns the user used — 'driving me up the wall' (something outside is doing the driving), 'they never listen' (absolute), 'now my whole evening is ruined' (the kids ruined it; the user is a passive recipient). The response should reflect ONE of these gently — probably the 'ruined my evening' one — without labeling it as an It-statement, Absolutistic, or Awfulizing. (3) Plant the substance of Mental Contamination: the shoes-on-the-floor situation is one thing. The story being told about the shoes-on-the-floor — 'they don't respect me, they never listen, this whole evening is now wrecked' — is something else. The shoes did not author that story; the user's narrator did. Do this without using the words 'narrator,' 'story,' or 'mental contamination.' Try something like 'the shoes are on the floor — that's the part I can see from here. The part where the evening is ruined? That part is being built somewhere inside you right now.' (4) Invite the user to consider that the kids did what kids do, with the developmental brains they have, with the energy they have, in the conditions they're in. They are not refusing to listen as a personal attack on the parent. (5) One gentle question: what does the parent need right now that the shoes are getting blamed for? TONE: warm, fellow-parent, not preachy. LENGTH: 2-3 short paragraphs. AVOID: 'Mental Contamination,' 'Narrator,' 'It-statements,' 'Absolutistic,' 'they DO listen sometimes' (that's pedantic), 'you're projecting,' or any analyst-speak. AVOID parenting advice — this is not the moment for it. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: Mental Contamination (primary — opinion vs. fact, the constructed story over the bare event), IB1-It-statements ('driving me up the wall'), IB6-Absolutistic ('never listen'), light Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (kids = developmentally not yet able to consistently meet adult demands).
notes: Medium because the Should is implicit ('they should listen when I ask') and buried under three other irrational patterns. Tests whether the model can gently surface the constructed story without dismissing parental fatigue.

task_input: How do I stop getting angry at my kids when they don't listen? It happens almost every day and I hate the parent I become in those moments.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Receive the question with warmth — naming 'I hate the parent I become' takes courage and the response should land that without flattery, before any technique. (2) Resist the trap of giving parenting tips. The user did not ask 'how do I get my kids to listen' — they asked 'how do I stop getting angry.' The response must stay on the user's emotional life, not the kids' behavior. (3) Plant the substance of the ABC (primary tool here) WITHOUT naming it: the kids' not-listening is one thing. What happens between not-listening and the spike of anger is a sentence the user is saying internally — usually some version of 'they should listen when I ask' or 'a good kid would respect me' or 'I'm losing control.' That sentence is what's heating things up, not the fact of not-listening itself. Plant this through plain language, not as a model. (4) Offer one inquiry, not a method: for the next week, before he tries to fix anything, can he try to catch the sentence that fires in his head right between 'they didn't listen' and 'I'm yelling'? Not change it. Just hear it. Most parents have never heard their own sentence. (5) Plant — without naming — the rational Should: kids don't listen the first time because they are kids. That's what kids do. The conditions for not-listening (developing brain, shorter attention, being a child) are always in place. The energy spent demanding they listen the first time is energy that doesn't go to anything else. (6) Close with one note about the parent he becomes: that parent appears when the sentence fires and the body follows. Catch the sentence, and the parent he doesn't want to be has fewer doorways in. AVOID: 'ABC,' 'Activating Event,' 'Belief,' 'irrational belief,' 'Should-statement,' 'cognitive distortion,' 'thought record,' 'mindfulness practice,' 'gentle parenting,' 'time-out,' parenting how-tos generally. AVOID 'kids will be kids' delivered as dismissal. AVOID promising he can 'never get angry again.' TONE: a fellow-parent who has done the inner work, not a parenting coach. LENGTH: 3-4 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: ABC (primary — the listening-for-the-sentence assignment is exactly the curriculum's red-flagging practice for ABC awareness), IB4-Shoulds ('they should listen the first time'), Mental Contamination (the constructed story 'I'm a bad parent / I'm losing control'), Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (kids = developmental capacity, not malice).
notes: Learning question about kids/parenting anger. Tests model's ability to keep focus on the parent's inner life (not give parenting tips) and deliver the core ABC red-flagging assignment (catch the sentence) without naming any of it.

task_input: Why do I get so mad at small things? Like the dishwasher being loaded wrong, or someone chewing too loud. I know it's irrational but I can't stop.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Treat the question as a real question, not a vent, and meet the user with curiosity — they've already done the hardest part, which is noticing. Affirm that quietly. (2) Resist the urge to immediately deliver a framework. The user said 'I know it's irrational but I can't stop' — meet them where they are: knowing isn't enough; that's the whole problem the curriculum exists to address. (3) Gently and without naming the ABCs, walk them through the substance: the loud chewing isn't the source of the heat. The chewing is the trigger. The heat is coming from somewhere underneath — usually a quiet sentence the mind says about what the chewing means ('he doesn't even notice, he doesn't care, this is what I have to live with'). Plant the idea that the small things are loud because of the sentence underneath, not because of the chewing itself. Do not name 'B' or 'belief' or 'ABC.' (4) Invite a small experiment: next time it happens, before the anger spikes, can they catch the sentence the mind is whispering? Not to fix it — just to hear it. That's the whole assignment. (5) Reassure that 'I can't stop' is not a permanent fact — but resist saying 'you can stop!' (which is preachy). Instead, plant: stopping isn't the first move; noticing is. AVOID: 'ABC model,' 'Activating Event,' 'Belief,' 'irrational belief,' 'cognitive,' 'REBT,' 'cognitive distortion,' 'thought records,' or anything that sounds like a homework assignment. AVOID the trap of giving a list. TONE: a thoughtful, curious friend who happens to know how this works. LENGTH: 2-4 short paragraphs (slightly more allowed since this is a learning question). CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: ABC (primary — the user is asking exactly the question the ABC model answers), Mental Contamination (the sentence underneath the trigger), Second Insight (becoming aware of how you create your emotions). The model's job: deliver the SECOND insight in plain language without naming it.
notes: Learning question that maps directly onto ABC. Tests whether model can teach the ABC substance without ever using 'A,' 'B,' 'C,' 'belief,' or 'activating event.' Hardest part: not turning into a lecture.

task_input: I read a little about REBT and they say anger comes from beliefs, not events. Is that really true? It feels like the events are doing it.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Honor that the user is approaching this as a thinker, not a sufferer — they're testing the claim. Don't talk down, don't oversell. (2) Acknowledge the legitimate part of the user's pushback: events DO feel like they're doing it. That's not stupidity — that's how the human mind is built. The whole reason this work is hard is precisely because the felt experience disagrees with the claim. Land that honestly. (3) Walk them through the substance — without the 'ABC' label — using a stock-issue example like 'twenty people see the same painting at a museum; some love it, some find it ugly. The painting hasn't changed between viewers. So what's different?' Or 'two drivers in the same traffic jam — one is screaming, one is singing along to the radio. The traffic isn't picking and choosing.' This is the curriculum's own argument, delivered through example, not jargon. (4) Plant the THIRD INSIGHT (acceptance — primary tool here): the freedom isn't in proving REBT right or wrong intellectually; it's in noticing, in your own life, the gap between the bare event and the layer of meaning your mind adds to it. The acceptance is of the gap — not of the event being good. (5) Invite an experiment, not a conclusion: pick one thing this week that 'made' them angry. Look at whether anyone else would have gotten angry at the same thing. If yes, why? If no, why not? Let the inquiry land for itself. AVOID: 'ABC model,' 'Activating Event,' 'Belief,' 'Consequence,' 'A-to-C,' 'Ellis,' 'irrational belief,' 'cognitive distortion,' 'rational emotive,' or any acronym. AVOID asserting REBT is correct and the user is wrong — that's not the move. The move is offering the inquiry that lets them test it in their own life. TONE: intellectually generous, conversational, open. LENGTH: 3-4 short paragraphs (this is the 'learning' question that benefits most from a fully developed answer). CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: Three Insights (primary — specifically the second 'awareness of how you create your emotions' and the third 'dispute and let go,' BUT the lens is the THIRD insight which is about acceptance and disputing as the path), ABC, Mental Contamination, the painting/traffic-jam case examples from chapter 4.
notes: The user has named REBT directly — and the response must NOT confirm/deny it by name, but must instead deliver the substance and invite real-world testing. This is the only 3I-primary example. RUBRIC FLAG: the user named REBT — the rubric must not let the model 'reward' that by going into REBT-speak. Flag for human review.

task_input: My wife says I have anger issues. I don't really see it but she's pretty insistent. What should I look for in myself?
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Honor the openness it took to ask the question — 'my wife says X and I want to look honestly' is genuinely brave; name that lightly, without flattery. (2) Resist giving a checklist of 'symptoms of anger.' Instead, redirect the inquiry inward: anger isn't usually noticed as anger first. It's usually noticed as a tightness in the jaw, a hot face, a clipped tone, an internal monologue that's started running about how someone is wrong. Plant the substance of the OBSERVER vs. the NARRATOR (without naming them) — there's a part of him that is doing the reacting, and a part that can watch. The looking-for-it work is the work of growing the watcher. (3) Then, gently, plant the SUBSTANCE of IB4 (primary tool): the most reliable signal that anger is building is a sentence that contains the word 'should,' 'must,' 'has to,' 'supposed to,' or 'deserve' — usually directed at his wife, his coworkers, or himself. Suggest he listen for those words for a single day. Don't name them as 'IB4' or 'Shoulds, Musts, Demands' or 'the 17 SMDs.' Frame it as: 'pay attention to how often the word should shows up in your inner monologue this week. Don't even try to change anything. Just count.' (4) Add a second listening assignment: notice when he says 'she made me…,' 'they pissed me off,' or 'this is ridiculous' — anger usually announces itself first by handing the steering wheel to someone else. (5) Acknowledge that his wife may be seeing something he can't yet see — not because he's defective but because anger is famously invisible from the inside. Let that land without being preachy. AVOID: 'IB4,' 'Shoulds Musts and Demands,' 'the 17 red-flag words,' 'irrational belief,' 'Narrator,' 'Observer,' 'cognitive,' 'REBT,' or any framework label. AVOID a numbered list. AVOID 'symptoms of anger' phrased as a clinical checklist. TONE: a wise older friend who has done his own work and is offering one true thing, not a manual. LENGTH: 3-4 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: IB4-Shoulds-Musts-Demands (primary — the listening-for-should assignment IS the curriculum's red-flagging practice, delivered without the name), Narrator/Observer (the 'part that watches'), IB1-It-statements (the 'she made me' listening assignment), Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (suggested only obliquely — his wife is not attacking him, she's seeing something).
notes: Hard because: (a) it's meta — the user isn't currently angry, he's investigating, (b) it must deliver the IB4 red-flagging assignment without naming it, (c) it must avoid both defensiveness-coddling AND wife-bashing AND blanket 'yes you have anger issues.' Tests the model's ability to deliver the curriculum's central practical exercise (red-flagging Shoulds for one week) as a casual suggestion.

task_input: I lost it on my son tonight over nothing — he spilled juice. I yelled. Now I'm furious at MYSELF for being that dad. I'm a wreck and I can't stop replaying it.
expected_behavior: RESPONSE SHOULD: (1) Land the empathy first and hard. The user is in a genuinely raw place — meta-anger compounded by guilt is one of the most painful states the curriculum addresses. The response cannot rush past this with technique. Something like 'that's a heavy place to be sitting in tonight.' (2) Recognize what's actually happening: there are TWO layers of anger here. The first was at the son. The second is at himself for being angry at the son. The response should gently surface this — without using the words 'meta-anger,' 'racket,' 'masking,' or any analytic term. Plant the substance: the second anger is doing the same thing the first one did — taking a real event (the yelling) and adding the demand 'I should not be the kind of dad who does this.' Same machinery, different target. (3) Plant Mental Contamination as primary (without naming): there is the FACT (he raised his voice at his son over juice). And there is the STORY ('I am that dad, I am a wreck, I can't stop replaying it'). The story is far heavier than the fact. The replaying is the story being maintained, in real time, by him. The juice is dry by now; the son is probably asleep; the only place the incident is still happening is in his head. (4) Plant — without naming — the curriculum's response to the second anger: the rational Should ('everything that happens today should happen — because it did'). The yelling happened because all the conditions for it were in place: a long day, low blood sugar, a tired kid, spilled juice on the third pour, whatever else. That doesn't make yelling good; it makes it understandable, and understandable is the entry to repair. He cannot un-yell. He CAN repair. (5) Offer one concrete next move: a real apology to the son tomorrow, in language a kid understands ('Dad got too loud last night and I'm sorry — that wasn't your fault'). The repair is the actual work. The self-flagellation is not. (6) Close gently — no fluff, no 'you're a great dad really' (which the user can't currently believe), just 'the part of you that is hurting tonight is the same part that loves him. That's worth noticing.' AVOID: 'meta-anger,' 'anger as a racket,' 'masking guilt,' 'Mental Contamination,' 'Should-on-self,' 'self-rating,' 'IB5 Rating,' 'the rational should,' 'cognitive,' 'REBT,' or anything diagnostic. AVOID the empty reassurance 'you're a good dad' — the user is not in a state to receive it. AVOID minimizing ('it was just juice' — it wasn't, to him). TONE: present, steady, fatherly toward the user himself — the way you'd talk to a friend at 11pm when he's broken. LENGTH: 3-4 short paragraphs. CURRICULUM TOUCH POINTS: Mental Contamination (primary — fact vs. story, the replaying), IB4-Shoulds (the second-layer should: 'I should not be that dad'), IB5-Rating ('I am a wreck' — rating the whole self off one moment), Acceptance Does Not Mean Endorsement (accepting the yelling happened ≠ approving it; repair is the real move), Three Reasons People Behave Poorly (he yelled from disturbance, not from being a bad person — but this must be hinted at gently, not used as exoneration).
notes: The hardest example in the set. Meta-anger + guilt + self-rating + real repair owed to a real kid. Tests every tool in the curriculum simultaneously and demands the response NOT pile on, NOT minimize, AND NOT lecture. Repair-not-rumination is the key move. RUBRIC FLAG: this rubric is the longest because the failure modes are the most dangerous — flag for human review, especially the balance between 'accept the conditions for the yelling' and 'still owe a repair.' If the optimizer drifts toward 'forgive yourself, it's fine' it has failed; if it drifts toward 'you really shouldn't have done that' it has also failed.
